coredata.dc:title: Construction of eco-friendly dual carbon dots ratiometric fluorescence probe for highly selective and efficient sensing mercury ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongli
Su, Xiaoyan
Liu, Huanjia
Zhu, Guifen
Ge, Guobei
Wang, Yuxin
Zhou, Penghui
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: In present work, blue carbon dots (b-CDs) were derived from ammonium citrate and guanidine hydrochloride, and red carbon dots (r-CDs) were stemmed from malonate, ethylenediamine and meso‑tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin based on facile hydrothermal method. Eco-friendly ratiometric fluorescence probe was innovatively constructed to effectively measure Hg2+ utilizing b-CDs and r-CDs. The developed probe displayed two typical emission peaks at 450 nm from b-CDs and 650 nm from r-CDs under the excitation at 360 nm. Mercury ion has strong quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm due to the electron transfer process and the fluorescence change at 450 nm was used as the response signal, whereas the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm kept unchangeable which resulted from the chemical inertness between Hg2+ and r-CDs, serving as the reference signal in the sensing system. Under optimal circumstances, this probe exhibited an excellent linearity between the fluorescence response values of ΔF450/F650 and Hg2+ concentrations over range of 0.01–10 µmol/L, and the limit of detection was down to 5.3 nmol/L. Furthermore, this probe was successfully employed for sensing Hg2+ in practical environmental water samples with satisfied recoveries of 98.5%–105.0%. The constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe provided a rapid, environmental-friendly, reliable, and efficient platform for measuring trace Hg2+ in environmental field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blue carbon dots
Red carbon dots
Electron transfer
Hg2+
Eco-friendliness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in environmental water samples
Characterization and optical properties of the ratiometric fluorescent probe

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22106039 , 21976211 , and 42007204 ), the Science Foundation of Henan Normal University (No. 2021PL23 ), the Excellent Science and Technology Innovation Team of Henan Normal University (No. 2021TD06), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in the University of Henan Province (No. 20IRTSTHN011).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis of the ratiometric fluorescent probe and the working principle for the detection of Hg2+.
(A) The TEM image of the b-CDs. (B) The histogram of size distribution for b-CDs. (C) The TEM image of the r-CDs. (D) The histogram of size distribution for r-CDs.

(A) XPS survey spectrum of b-CDs. (B) High resolution XPS spectrum of C1s of b-CDs. (C) High resolution XPS spectrum of N1s of b-CDs. (D) High resolution XPS spectrum of O1s of b-CDs. (E) XPS survey spectrum of r-CDs (F) High resolution XPS spectrum of C1s of r-CDs. (G) High resolution XPS spectrum of N1s of r-CDs. (H) High resolution XPS spectrum of O1s of r-CDs.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of b-CDs, r-CDs, b/r-CDs, b-CDs+Hg2+, r-CDs+Hg2+, and b/r-CDs+Hg2+. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Hg2+ (black line), b-CDs (red line), and b-CDs+Hg2+ (blue line). (C) The fluorescence emission spectrum (black line) and excitation spectrum (red line) of the b-CDs, and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Hg2+ (blue line). (D) Zeta potentials of the b-CDs, b-CDs+Hg2+, b/r-CDs, and b/r-CDs+Hg2+. (E) Fluorescence decay curves of b-CDs and b-CDs+Hg2+. (F) Fluorescence decay curves of b/r-CDs and b/r -CDs+Hg2+.

(A) The fluorescence responses of the ratiometric fluorescent probe upon the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+. (B) A linear correlation of ΔF450/F650 values versus the concentrations of Hg2+ in the range of 0.01–10 µmol/L.

The selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ over other metal ions (single) and the mixture of Hg2+ with other metal ions (mixture, metal ions concentration: 0.05 µmol/L of Hg2+, 1.5 µmol/L of Cr6+, 5.0 µmol/L of Fe3+, 1.0 µmol/L of Pb2+, 1.0 µmol/L of Cd2+, 1.0 µmol/L of Cr3+, 0.5 µmol/L of Fe2+, 0.5 µmol/L of Cu2+, 0.5 µmol/L of Mg2+, 0.2 µmol/L of Co2+, 5.0 µmol/L of K +, 5.0 µmol/L of Na+, 5.0 µmol/L of Ba2+, 1.0 µmol/L of Zn2+, 5.0 µmol/L of Mn2+, 5.0 µmol/L of Ca2+, 5.0 µmol/L of Ni2+).
Comparison of different ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+.
Spiked recoveries for Hg2+ in environmental samples.

head.ce:title: Construction of eco-friendly dual carbon dots ratiometric fluorescence probe for highly selective and efficient sensing mercury ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control
Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In present work, blue carbon dots (b-CDs) were derived from ammonium citrate and guanidine hydrochloride, and red carbon dots (r-CDs) were stemmed from malonate, ethylenediamine and meso‑tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin based on facile hydrothermal method. Eco-friendly ratiometric fluorescence probe was innovatively constructed to effectively measure Hg2+ utilizing b-CDs and r-CDs. The developed probe displayed two typical emission peaks at 450 nm from b-CDs and 650 nm from r-CDs under the excitation at 360 nm. Mercury ion has strong quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm due to the electron transfer process and the fluorescence change at 450 nm was used as the response signal, whereas the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm kept unchangeable which resulted from the chemical inertness between Hg2+ and r-CDs, serving as the reference signal in the sensing system. Under optimal circumstances, this probe exhibited an excellent linearity between the fluorescence response values of ΔF450/F650 and Hg2+ concentrations over range of 0.01–10 µmol/L, and the limit of detection was down to 5.3 nmol/L. Furthermore, this probe was successfully employed for sensing Hg2+ in practical environmental water samples with satisfied recoveries of 98.5%–105.0%. The constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe provided a rapid, environmental-friendly, reliable, and efficient platform for measuring trace Hg2+ in environmental field.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) is a prevalent and extremely harmful from of heavy metal contamination found in various media especially in water, and can lead to a range of illnesses even at trace levels owing to its direct toxicity and bioaccumulation, whose treats to human health and ecological environment have aroused widespread concerns (Han et al., 2024; Song et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022a; Zhang et al., 2022). Considerable endeavors have been made to acquire numerous techniques for Hg2+ detection, which include atomic absorption spectrometry (Li et al., 2020), atomic emission spectrometry (Yuan et al., 2020), capacitive coupled plasma microemission spectrometry (Angyus et al., 2020). Although the above approaches are highly sensitive and selective, they also have some intrinsic shortages of expensive instruments, long testing time, and sophisticated operational procedure, which greatly limit the application for effective monitoring (Sonowal and Saikia, 2023; Zhou et al., 2021). Therefore, it is urgent and imperative to explore some novel simple, convenient, economic, and efficient approaches for sensing Hg2+.

Peer-reviewed literatures reveal that fluorescence analysis has become a rapid, effective, sensitive, and widespread method for detecting pollutants, which is attributed to its notable benefits of simplicity, convenience, rapid response, high sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness (Liu et al., 2021; Sadhanala et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2021; Yadav et al., 2023). However, most of fluorescence probes rely on a single read-out signal, and they are susceptible to environmental conditions, and are unstable in complicated samples, which may enhance the detection errors and has become hurdles of their practical applications to some extent (He et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2022). In consequence, this impels the emergence of ratiometric fluorescence probes, which have been rapidly progressed in recent years. In general, ratiometric fluorescence probes possess two distinct emission peaks that vary differently in response to the analyte. External factors can be eliminated through determining the ratio of these two peaks, resulting in a more stable and accurate measurement (Chen et al., 2021a; Liu et al., 2023b; Xie et al., 2018). Consequently, the field of fluorescence analysis has witnessed an increasing interest in ratiometric fluorescent probes. Particularly, the construction and preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probes continues to be a difficult task, necessitating the incorporation of two distinct emission peaks within a unified framework (one emission peak serving as the responsive indicator and the other as the comparative indicator).

Up to now, various fluorescent materials have been explored and designed for ratiometric fluorescent probes, involving metal nanoclusters, semiconductor quantum dots, rare earth elements, and carbon dots (Li et al., 2019, 2023a; Liu et al., 2023a). Chu et al. constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe utilizing ZnS quantum dots incorporated with rare earth elements (ZnS:Ce QDs) for highly selective measuring Hg2+ with a detection limit of 820 nmol/L (Chu et al., 2020). In the presence of Hg2+, ZnS:Ce QDs preferred to adsorb Hg2+ and part of ZnS was changed to HgS, which made the ZnS:Ce QDs surface structure change and aggregate, further leading to the fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the probe was applied for sensing Hg2+ in tap water. Another ratiometric fluorescence probe was proposed by Liu and coworkers to detect Hg2+ based on the dynamic quenching mechanism (Liu et al., 2019). The fluorescence of gold nanoclusters around 665 nm was drastically quenched by Hg2+ whereas that of carbon dots at 450 nm kept constant. Good linearity was found ranging from 0.1 to 90 µmol/L with limit of detection as 63 nmol/L. Guo et al. (2021) designed a unique ratiometric fluorescent probe by encapsulating carbon dots into the chamber of europium metal-organic frameworks for highly sensitive analysis of Hg2+ in real water samples. The fluorescence of carbon dots at 430 nm was reduced upon the introduction of Hg2+, while that of europium metal-organic frameworks at 614 nm hardly changed. Regrettably, most of the above probes were based on quantum dots, noble metal-based nanoclusters, and rare earth elements, which were strong toxicity, poor water dispersibilities, high cost, and low biocompatibilities, and the complex preparation restricted their further applications (Xu et al., 2019). Meanwhile, the sensitivities needed to be improved for their applications for trace Hg2+ analysis in real environmental samples. Therefore, it is crucial to establish a viable pathway for the uncomplicated synthesis of ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibiting dual emission.

Lately, there has been an increasing utilization of carbon dots (CDs) in the construction of innovative ratiometric fluorescent probes, which was owing to their remarkable characteristics including convenient synthesis, diverse carbon sources, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and abundant surface functional groups (Chen et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2022, 2023b). Particularly, it is still more desirable to explore less complex, cost-effective, and effective ratiometric fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+ using environmentally friendly CDs, which eliminate the requirement for modifying CDs afterwards or combining them with different types of fluorescent substances. Wang and co-workers designed another ratiometric fluorescent probe to sense Hg2+ on the basis of dual-emission CDs by using polyetherimide, citric acid, and Rhodamine B as the precursors (Wang et al., 2020a). Long et al. (2020) developed a novel type of CDs using powdered cabbage with two emissions at 500 nm and 678 nm. The fluorescence emission of CDs at 678 nm possessed selective and rapid response to Hg2+ owing to the static quenching, while that at 500 nm was served as the reference signal. This probe exhibited good linearity over range of 0.01–5 µmol/L. The aforementioned ratiometric fluorescent probes possessed the advantages of simplicity and high sensitivity. Up to now, the ratiometric fluorescent probes derived from two kinds of CDs are very rare.

In this study, an eco-friendly ratiometric fluorescent probe utilizing blue/red CDs was proposed for rapid and efficient determination of Hg2+. The synthesis of the probe and the operational principle for detecting Hg2+ was demonstrated in Scheme 1 . The blue CDs (b-CDs) were created from ammonium citrate and guanidine hydrochloride, while the red CDs (r-CDs) were fabricated by using malonate, meso‑tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin, and ethylenediamine as the carbon sources. The properties of b-CDs and r-CDs were well characterized and analyzed via TEM, UV–vis, FL, FT-IR, XPS and zeta potential analysis.

Ammonium citrate (98.5%), meso‑tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin (97%), malonate (99.5%), and guanidine hydrochloride (99.5%) were bought from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Ethylenediamine (99.5%) was from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (36%) and sodium hydroxide (99.5%) were obtained from Tianjin De'en Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. Mercury chloride, nickel chloride, manganese chloride, ferric chloride, magnesium nitrate, cupric nitrate, lead nitrate, cadmium nitrate, and chromic nitrate were from Beijing Chemical Factory. Dialysis bags (MWCO: 100–500, 500–1000) were gained from SpectrumLabs Inc.

The sizes and morphologies of the ratiometric fluorescence probe were described by a TEM (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). FT-IR spectra were characterized using the Infrared spectrometer (Spectrum 400F, PerkinElmer, USA). XPS spectra were performed on the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were performed on a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS 980, Edinburgh, UK). Fluorescence spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian, USA). UV–vis spectra were measured on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (T6, Pgeneral, China).

Both the b-CDs and r-CDs were fabricated by a simple hydrothermal approach. In brief, 0.5 g ammonium citrate and 1.0 g guanidine hydrochloride were mixed in 20 mL sodium hydroxide solution (1 mol/L). The solution was heated at 200 °C in the polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle for 3 hr. After cooling naturally, the 0.22 µm membrane was utilized to filter out large particles and impurities, and the solution was further dialyzed with the dialysis bag (MWCO: 100–500 Da) for 3 hr. The purified b-CDs solution was dried at 60 °C. Finally, the acquired b-CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) was kept at 4 °C for the following experiments.

The r-CDs were synthesized in the following: 5.2 mg of malonate, 39.5 mg of meso‑tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin, and 300 µL ethylenediamine were mixed with 10 mL of ultrapure water. The solution underwent heating in an oven for 12 hr at 200 °C. Subsequently, the solution was filtered using the 0.22 µm membrane to get rid of the impurities and large particles. The dialysis bag (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) was used to perform dialysis for 24 hr, and the r-CDs solution (0.15 mg/mL) was kept at 4 °C for further utilization.

The ratiometric fluorescent analysis of Hg2+ was conducted at room temperature in ultrapure water. In typical detection experiments, b-CDs solution (64 µL, 0.5 mg/mL), r-CDs solution (100 µL, 0.15 mg/mL), and various concentrations of Hg2+ solution were introduced into centrifuge tubes, and were then diluted with ultrapure water to reach the final volume of 2 mL, respectively. Next, the mixture solution was put into the quartz cuvette for quantitative detection. The fluorescent spectra were measured when the ratiometric fluorescent probe was excited at 360 nm with both slits of 5 nm. The experiments were conducted in triplicate.

To assess the specificity of this ratiometric fluorescent probe towards Hg2+, a range of metal ion solutions were prepared, which included Cr6+ (0.1–1.5 µmol/L), Fe3+ (0.1–1.5 µmol/L), Pb2+ (0.1–1.0 µmol/L), Cd2+ (0.1–1.0 µmol/L), Cr3+ (0.1–1.0 µmol/L), Fe2+ (0.1–0.5 µmol/L), Cu2+ (0.1–0.5 µmol/L), Mg2+ (0.1–0.5 µmol/L), Co2+ (0.1–0.2 µmol/L), K+ (1.0–5.0 µmol/L), Na+ (1.0–5.0 µmol/L), Ba2+ (0.1–5.0 µmol/L), Zn2+ (0.1–1.0 µmol/L), Mn2+ (0.1–5.0 µmol/L), Ca2+ (0.1–5.0 µmol/L), and Ni2+ (1.0–5.0 µmol/L). Likewise, the ΔF450/F650 of the ratiometric fluorescent probe exposed to the sixteen metal ions in the single solution and mixture with Hg2+ were further investigated according to the same process as Hg2+ detection. Triplicate experiments were conducted in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three environmental water samples including Yangtze river, rain water, and tap water were collected for estimating the practicability and feasibility of this constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting Hg2+. Pretreatment of these environmental water samples were conducted through filtering using the 0.22 µm microporous membranes to get rid of insoluble substances. These pretreated samples were determined at the blank and spiked concentrations (0.05 µmol/L, 0.10 µmol/L, and 0.50 µmol/L). The analysis procedure was the same as the above-mentioned Hg2+ detection. These experiments were repeated at five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and size distribution of b-CDs and r-CDs were obtained by the TEM and demonstrated in Fig. 1A-D . As revealed in Fig. 1A, the prepared b-CDs generally exhibited uniform spherical nanostructures with good dispersibility, and the diameters mostly fell within the range of 1.8–3.2 nm with the mean particle size of approximately 2.50 nm (Fig. 1B). As seen from Fig. 1C, the r-CDs possessed nearly spherical morphologies and high monodispersity. The diameters of r-CDs varied between 1.5 nm and 1.8 nm, and the average size was 1.59 nm (Fig. 1D). As displayed in Appendix A Figs. S1A and S1B, both b-CDs and r-CDs had distinct lattice fringes and the lattice spaces were 0.199 nm and 0.20 nm, respectively, indicating the existence of graphite carbon in both two kinds of CDs (Ran et al., 2022).

The surface functional groups of the ratiometric fluorescent probe were investigated via the FT-IR spectrometry. The typical peaks centered around 3446 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration), 1587 cm−1 (N–H bending vibration), and 1625 cm−1 (C=O stretching vibration) indicated the existence of amine bond functional groups at the b-CDs surface (Appendix A Fig. S1C). The O–H stretching vibration was assigned to another peak at approximately 3276 cm−1. Moreover, two typical peaks at 2937 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1 were the stretching vibration of C–H. Peaks around 1392 cm−1 and 1290 cm−1 were derived from the C–H stretching vibration and the C–N bending vibration, respectively (Ye et al., 2020). As demonstrated in Appendix A Fig. S1D, the distinctive peaks around 3047 cm−1 and 1529 cm−1 were contributed to the stretching and deformation vibration of N–H, respectively. Two peaks around 2923 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 indicated the existence of C–H. The four peaks at 1644 cm−1, 1587 cm−1, 1374 cm−1, and 1093 cm−1 were ascribed to the C=O, C=N, C–N, and C–O functional groups at the r-CDs surface (Wu et al., 2020).

XPS were performed to further investigate the structure and chemical compositions of the ratiometric fluorescent probe. The survey scan of b-CDs in Fig. 2A presented three peaks at 285.0 eV, 399.0 eV and 531.0 eV, which could be assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. As revealed in Fig. 2B, the high resolution C1s spectrum of b-CDs showed four feature peaks around 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV, 287.8 eV, and 289.0 eV. These peaks were ascribed to the C–C/C=C, C–O, C=N, and C=O, respectively (Yang et al., 2014). The N1s spectrum of b-CDs consisted of C=N (399.4 eV) and –NH2 (400.1 eV) peaks (Fig. 2C) (Ye et al., 2020). The O1s spectrum of b-CDs exhibited there were three different peaks around 530.8 eV, 532.0 eV, and 535.2 eV (Fig. 2D), that were corresponded to the C=O, C–OH/C–O–C, and C–O bonds, respectively (Wang et al., 2021). The presence of different kinds of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen bonds suggested the presence of different C, N, and O states on the b-CDs surface. Furthermore, the survey scan of r-CDs in Fig. 2E presented three peaks around 285.0 eV, 400.0 eV and 532.0 eV, which could be associated to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. As observed in Fig. 2F, four distinct characteristic peaks of C1s spectrum centered at 284.4 eV, 285.0 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.2 eV were associated with C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N, C=N, and C=O, which indicated the existence of conjugated structure in r-CDs. Fig. 2G shows three binding energies around 397.7 eV (pyridinic N), 399.6 eV (pyrrolic N), and 401.2 eV (–NH2) in N1s spectrum, which demonstrated that some nitrogen atoms had been successfully doped into the carbon framework and other nitrogen atoms existed in the form of amino groups on the r-CDs surface. Two distinct peaks at approximately 531.4 eV and 533.1 eV were observed (Fig. 2H), which accurately matched the O1s spectrum, and were due to C=O and C–O (Wu et al., 2020). These results revealed that the XPS data were consistent with the FT-IR results, and there were various hydrophilic functional groups involving carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface of the prepared ratiometric fluorescent probes. These functional groups not only increased the water solubility, but also improved the fluorescent performance of the constructed fluorescent probe (Liu et al., 2021).

Both UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were performed to investigate the optical properties of b-CDs and r-CDs. The shoulder absorption peak around 235 nm in Appendix A Fig. S2A was resulted from the π-π* transition of C=C conjugate hybridization, and another apparent peak around 345 nm was related to the n-π* transition of C=O on the b-CDs surface (Chen et al., 2018). The maximum emission wavelength of b-CDs was emerged at 448 nm, while the optimal excitation wavelength was found to be 345 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength of b-CDs was close to the ultraviolet absorption at 340 nm, which was originated from the surface state or radiative transition of b-CDs (Kim et al., 2021). Meanwhile, the b-CDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 51.3%, with quinine sulfate (having a quantum yield of 54%) used as a reference (Liu et al., 2017), which also provided the excellent optical performance for b-CDs. It was apparent from Appendix A Fig. S2B that the obtained b-CDs possessed emission behavior that was not dependent on excitation, as the corresponding emission peaks remained around 448 nm with no visible shifts as the excitation wavelengths changing from 305 nm to 385 nm. This characteristic might be closely attributed to the consistent size of the b-CDs, as observed in the TEM findings (Liu et al., 2022). Appendix A Fig. S2C depicted the two strong absorption peaks (blue line) of r-CDs around 229 nm and 413 nm, representing the π-π* transition of C=C and the n-π* transition of C=O of the surface functional groups. The fluorescence spectra of r-CDs demonstrated the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths were around 410 nm and 650 nm, and the maximum excitation wavelength was close to the ultraviolet absorption peak at 413 nm, indicating that the luminescence of r-CDs was relevant to the molecular state (Wu et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the gained r-CDs also owned the typical excitation-independent emission property (Appendix A Fig. S2D), because the relevant emission peaks still stayed at 650 nm without apparent shifts as the excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 nm to 425 nm, which might originate from the relatively narrow size distribution.

Ratiometric fluorescent probes require a high degree of stability to be effective. Therefore, the impact of pH and ionic strength on the fluorescence intensities of this probe were further investigated and demonstrated in Appendix A Figs. S3A and S3B, respectively. The constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe possessed steady values of F450/F650 over the pH range of 5–12 with inconspicuous change. However, the values of F450/F650 increased as the pH increase when the pH was above 12. In addition, when the pH fell within 1–4 range, the fluorescence intensities of two kinds of CDs were both quenched under strongly acidic conditions, and the values of F450/F650 were close to zero upon the given concentration, which was probably attributed to the influence of protonation-deprotonation on the diverse surface functional groups of b-CDs and r-CDs (Wang et al., 2019). The values of F450/F650 kept negligible change as the ionic strength increase in the NaCl concentrations range of 0–0.1 mol/L, indicating this probe earned good optical stability and potential applications under physiological conditions (Ren et al., 2017). Furthermore, it was notable from Appendix A Fig. S3C that the values of F450/F650 of the probe remained constant, and were rather stable within one month, suggesting that the synthesized probe not only possessed good fluorescence stability but also was advantageous for effective analysis. Above all, the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibited fabulous photostability, and could be utilized as a novel high-performance fluorescent probe.

As observed in Fig. 3A , Hg2+ significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs, whereas the fluorescence intensity of r-CDs kept stable. Moreover, there were two emission peaks in the mixed solution derived from b-CDs and r-CDs, and it was as expected that only the fluorescence from b-CDs was dramatically quenched while other emission remained, exhibiting an obvious ratiometric response. Therefore, a novel and eco-friendly ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed by utilizing b-CDs as the fluorescence response indicator and r-CDs as the reference indicator. In general, the quenching mechanisms mostly involve dynamic quenching, static quenching, inner filter effect, Förster resonance energy transfer, electron transfer, and so on (Wang et al., 2022b; Zu et al., 2017). A series of measurements including absorption spectra, zeta potentials and fluorescence lifetimes were carried out to explore the quenching mechanism. As exhibited in Fig. 3B, there were no different absorption peaks and obvious changes compared to the UV–vis absorption spectra of b-CDs, Hg2+, and b-CDs+Hg2+, which indicated b-CDs and Hg2+ was not chemically reacting with each other, and no complex was formed between them. The result eliminated the static quenching, which possessed distinct variations in the UV–vis absorption spectra of fluorescent probe with the existence of analyte resulted from the formation of the ground-state complex (Liu et al., 2021; Zu et al., 2017). Additionally, the quenching will occur through the inner filter effect or Förster resonance energy transfer while the emission or excitation spectra of fluorescent probe overlap with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the analyte (Hu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020b). As revealed in Fig. 3C, there was no overlap between the absorption spectrum of Hg2+ and the emission or excitation spectra of b-CDs, that indicates that the quenching mechanism was not owing to the inner filter effect or Förster resonance energy transfer. Meanwhile, with the introduction of Hg2+, the zeta potential of b-CDs underwent a change from –9.16 to –4.51 mV, and the zeta potential of b/r-CDs solution also increased remarkably from −25.1 to −21.6 mV in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. 3D), suggesting that the electron transfer might occur between b-CDs and Hg2+ (Pandey et al., 2020). What's more, the conclusion was further verified through the fluorescence lifetime measurements. The fluorescence lifetimes of b-CDs and b-CDs+Hg2+ were 7.37 ns and 6.36 ns, and those of b/r-CDs and b/r-CDs+Hg2+ were 9.17 ns and 8.12 ns, respectively. The apparent fluctuations of more than 1 ns were displayed in Fig. 3E and 3F, and those strongly proved the occurrence of effective electron transfer process between b-CDs and Hg2+ (Liu et al., 2022). All the above-mentioned results revealed the fluorescence quenching was dominantly derived from the electron transfer.

Furthermore, systematic experiments were designed and performed to optimize several vital parameters involving the probe concentration, pH value, and reaction time to gain better detection performance. In general, with the given concentration of Hg2+, the better analysis performance and greater sensitivity can be obtained with a lower probe concentration, while a wider determination range may be acquired at a higher probe concentration. Hence, the analytic process desperately needed the appropriate probe concentration. As the response signal of the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe, the b-CDs concentration contributed significantly for the analysis and was optimized. Appendix A Fig. S4A displays that the results were significantly affected by the b-CDs concentration, and the ΔF450/F650 values initially enhanced with the Hg2+ concentrations from 12 µg/mL to 16 µg/mL, which was related to the more interaction between Hg2+ and b-CDs, and later declined as the continuous increase of b-CDs concentrations, which was attributed to the fact that more b-CDs required more Hg2+ for reacting. The maximum value of ΔF450/F650 was achieved with the b-CDs concentration of 16 µg/mL. Therefore, 16 µg/mL was the optimal concentration for detecting Hg2+ in further trials. As illustrated in Appendix A Fig. S4B, the ΔF450/F650 values firstly enhanced remarkably as pH varying from pH 5 to pH 7, and later declined with the consistent increase of pH value. Under a more acidic environment, hydrogen ions could potentially interact with amino groups of b-CDs, which weakened the reaction between b-CDs and Hg2+, and further lessen the quenching effect. Upon intense alkaline conditions, there was a rapid hydrolysis of Hg2+, which led to the attenuation of the interaction between b-CDs and Hg2+ and a reduction in the quenching impact (Liu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019). So, the proper pH was pH 7 for follow-up detection experiments. Additionally, as depicted in Appendix A Fig. S4C, the values of ΔF450/F650 reached equilibrium within 2 min and remained stable within 10 min, which suggested that this probe could achieve the efficient and rapid determination of Hg2+. Therefore, 2 min was deemed appropriate for subsequent analyses.

As described above, the fluorescent intensity of b-CDs was significantly quenched by Hg2+ whereas that of r-CDs kept stable, indicating that it might be possible to develop a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe to quantitatively detect Hg2+ by combing the b-CDs and r-CDs. To investigate the sensitivity of the probe upon the optimum detection circumstances, various concentrations of Hg2+ were put to the probe solution and the fluorescence intensities of two emission peaks were further examined. As demonstrated in Fig. 4A , the blue fluorescence at 450 nm decreased progressively accompanying with consistent red fluorescence at 650 nm as the Hg2+ concentrations increasing from 0 to 10 µmol/L. An excellent linear relationship was found between ΔF450/F650 values and Hg2+ concentration (0.01–10 µmol/L). Additionally, the linear regression equation was ΔF450/F650 = 0.1972C + 0.1063, and the correlation coefficient was 0.9990. Here, C represented the Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 4B). What's more, the impressive limit of detection (LOD) was 5.3 nmol/L(3σ/s), which was much lower than the maximum residue limit of 10 nmol/L for Hg2+ in drinking water by US Environmental Protection Agency. Zhang et al. (2022) introduced a new ratiometric fluorescent probe (CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8) by encapsulating CDs and InP/ZnS quantum dots (InPQDs) into the MOF of ZIF-8 to realize precise detection of Hg2+ with exceptional selectivity. This was the first work to apply InPQDs as the response signal to detect Hg2+, and this probe exhibited a LOD of 8.68 nmol/L with a linear range of 0–0.5 µmol/L. This method had a low LOD, nevertheless, the correlative materials and reagents were somewhat toxic, and the detection range was comparatively narrow. Wang et al. (2020a) constructed another novel type of dual-emission CDs to sense Hg2+ through a facile hydrothermal pyrolysis. The approach was comparatively simple and eco-friendly, but it possessed a higher LOD of 240 nmol/L, which was unfavorable for the detection of Hg2+ at low concentrations. Another ratiometric fluorescent probe were prepared with two emissions at 500 nm and 678 nm through one-pot solvothermal approach (Long et al., 2020). The linear range was over the range of 0.01–5 µmol/L with LOD of 6.25 nmol/L. Nevertheless, the preparation procedure required organic reagents. Shu and co-workers proposed another novel analytical method to effectively detect Hg2+ based on the ratiometric fluorescent probe (CsPbBr3@Eu-BTC) via the solvothermal method (Shu et al., 2022). The green fluorescence of CsPbBr3 was evidently quenched resulted from the electron transfer, and the red fluorescence of Eu3+ was maintained upon the addition of Hg2+. The measurement range was from 0 µmol/L to 1 µmol/L with a lower LOD of 0.116 nmol/L. However, the preparation process was cumbersome and time-consuming, meanwhile, organic reagents including N,N-dimethylformamide and toluene were involved in the preparation process, which created hazards to humans and the environment to some extent. What's more, a comparison was performed and the corresponding results were depicted in Table 1 . In contrast to other previous reports, the constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibited an excellent analytical performance and was capable of offering a higher analytical sensitivity and a broader linear range. Moreover, it offered cost-effectiveness, simplicity, efficiency, and environmental compatibility.

As demonstrated in the previous experiments, the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe could be utilized to sense Hg2+ with high sensitivity. Nonetheless, the primary challenges for Hg2+ determination were not simply a matter of sensitivity, but also more crucially selectivity because of the complexities of the environmental water samples. Thus, relevant experiments were performed to estimate the selectivity of this probe towards Hg2+. Firstly, a variety of 17 metal ions including Hg2+ (0.05 µmol/L), Co2+ (0.2 µmol/L), Fe2+ (0.5 µmol/L), Cu2+ (0.5 µmol/L), Mg2+ (0.5 µmol/L), Pb2+ (1.0 µmol/L), Cd2+(1.0 µmol/L), Zn2+(1.0 µmol/L), Cr3+ (1.0 µmol/L), Cr6+ (1.5 µmol/L), Fe3+ (5.0 µmol/L), K + (5.0 µmol/L), Na+ (5.0 µmol/L), Ba2+ (5.0 µmol/L), Mn2+ (5.0 µmol/L), Ca2+ (5.0 µmol/L), Ni2+ (5.0 µmol/L) were individually added into the probe solution. Subsequently, the changes of fluorescence intensities were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5 (single), only Hg2+ possessed a strong quenching effect on the probe, and other interferences caused inapparent fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, mixtures of Hg2+ and the aforementioned interferences were put into the solution, and the relevant fluorescence changes were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (mixture), the existence of the above interfering metal ions possessed insignificant impact on the fluorescence responses of this probe, suggesting Hg2+ owned a closer and faster interaction with the probe in contrast to other interferences. The results powerfully demonstrated the constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe had an outstanding selectivity and could be used to specifically recognize Hg2+.

A series of experiments including intra-day and inter-day precisions, stability studies were further performed at three Hg2+ concentrations to evaluate the reliability of the newly proposed probe. The fluorescent responses for Hg2+ of this probe were measured six times a day to determinate the intra-day precision, and those for inter-day precisions were further measured one time per day over six consecutive days. As depicted in Appendix A Table S1, the relative standard deviation (RSD) values of intra-day precision with three levels of 0.05, 0.10, and 0.50 µmol/L were 1.1%, 1.3%, 1.2%, respectively. Likewise, the RSD values of inter-day precision with the same concentrations were 1.0%, 2.1%, and 1.4%, respectively. All RSD values were less than 2.1%, which indicated the probe owned excellent precisions. The stability of this probe was assessed by detecting the fluorescent responses of probe solution fortified Hg2+ with the above three concentrations within 30 days. As exhibited in Appendix A Fig. S5, only negligible changes were observed. These gained results revealed the designed probe possessed outstanding reliability and stability for determination of Hg2+.

To evaluate and verify the practicability and feasibility of the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe, the ratiometric fluorescent probe was utilized to measure Hg2+ in the bank and spiked environmental water samples. As displayed in Table 2 , no Hg2+ was found in these practical water samples. Furthermore, various dosages of Hg2+ were introduced to the environmental samples during the spiked trials. The average recoveries of Hg2+ in three samples ranged from 98.5% to 105.0%, and the RSD values were less than 4.7%. These obtained results clearly revealed that the developed probe offered good accuracy and precision. Consequently, this constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe could be a considerable promising competitor for monitoring Hg2+ in environmental water samples.

In this work, b-CDs from ammonium citrate and guanidine hydrochloride, and r-CDs from meso‑tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphin, malonate and ethylenediamine were successfully prepared through the facile hydrothermal approach. An efficient and reliable ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed based on eco-friendly b-CDs and r-CDs without further complicated aftertreatment to sensitively and selectively detect Hg2+. The proposed probe possessed a wide linear range of 0.01–10 µmol/L and a low LOD of 5.3 nmol/L under optimum conditions. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of b-CDs by Hg2+ was noticeably ascribed to the electron transfer process. Furthermore, this ratiometric fluorescent probe was resoundingly utilized to measure Hg2+ in environmental water samples with satisfied precisions and recoveries. The constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe can be easily prepared, and exhibited excellent selectivity, and high sensitivity, environmental friendliness. Therefore, this developed probe is economic, convenient, rapid, environmentally friendly, and effective for determination of Hg2+ and which will expand its potential application in environmental and related fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22106039, 21976211, and 42007204), the Science Foundation of Henan Normal University (No. 2021PL23), the Excellent Science and Technology Innovation Team of Henan Normal University (No. 2021TD06), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in the University of Henan Province (No. 20IRTSTHN011).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jes.2024.01.013.
